worst_over = scatter_df.nsmallest(1, "residual_A")[["WardName", "Borough", "residual_A"]]
print(worst_over)

    WardName  Borough  residual_A
577   Darwin  Bromley   -2.759068
In [42]:
# ── Build map_gdf ────────────────────────────────────────────────
map_gdf = ward_gdf[["WardCode", "WardName", "Borough", "geometry"]].copy()
map_gdf = map_gdf.merge(
    feature_matrix[["WardCode", "typeA_density_log"]],
    on="WardCode", how="left"
)
map_gdf["typeA_pred_log"] = rf_models["Type A — Person/Society"].predict(
    feature_matrix[feature_cols].fillna(0)
)
map_gdf["residual_A"] = (
    map_gdf["typeA_density_log"] - map_gdf["typeA_pred_log"]
).round(3)

# Remove Aviation Security artefact
map_gdf = map_gdf[
    ~map_gdf["Borough"].str.contains('Aviation|SO18', case=False, na=False)
].copy()

map_gdf = map_gdf.to_crs("EPSG:27700")
gla_plot = gla_gdf.to_crs("EPSG:27700")

# Colormap and norm
from matplotlib.colors import TwoSlopeNorm, LinearSegmentedColormap
div_cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    'residual', ['#2C3B4D', '#F7F4ED', '#A35139']
)
vmax = map_gdf["residual_A"].abs().quantile(0.95)
norm = TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=-vmax, vcenter=0, vmax=vmax)
In [43]:
import geopandas as gpd

# ── Borough boundaries ───────────────────────────────────────────
# Dissolve wards to borough level
borough_gdf = map_gdf.dissolve(by='Borough').reset_index()

# Get top underpredicted boroughs by mean residual
top_under = (
    map_gdf.groupby('Borough')['residual_A']
    .mean()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(5)
    .index.tolist()
)

top_over = (
    map_gdf.groupby('Borough')['residual_A']
    .mean()
    .sort_values(ascending=True)
    .head(5)
    .index.tolist()
)

# ── Plot ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8), facecolor='#F7F4ED')
ax.set_facecolor('#F7F4ED')

gla_plot.plot(ax=ax, color='#D8D3C8', edgecolor='none')

map_gdf.plot(
    column='residual_A',
    cmap=div_cmap,
    norm=norm,
    legend=True,
    linewidth=0.1,
    edgecolor='#C9C1B1',
    legend_kwds={
        'shrink':  0.6,
        'aspect':  25,
        'pad':     0.02,
        'format':  '%.1f',
        'label':   'Residual'
    },
    ax=ax,
    missing_kwds={'color': '#C9C1B1'}
)

gla_plot.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color='#1B2632', linewidth=1.0)

# ── Highlight top underpredicted boroughs ────────────────────────
under_gdf = borough_gdf[borough_gdf['Borough'].isin(top_under)]
over_gdf  = borough_gdf[borough_gdf['Borough'].isin(top_over)]

under_gdf.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax, color='#A35139', linewidth=1.8, linestyle='--'
)
over_gdf.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax, color='#2C3B4D', linewidth=1.8, linestyle='--'
)

# ── Borough centroid labels ──────────────────────────────────────
for _, row in under_gdf.iterrows():
    cx, cy = row.geometry.centroid.x, row.geometry.centroid.y
    ax.annotate(
        row['Borough'],
        xy=(cx, cy),
        fontsize=7, color='#A35139',
        fontweight='bold', ha='center', va='center',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.2',
                  facecolor='#F7F4ED',
                  edgecolor='none', alpha=0.7)
    )

for _, row in over_gdf.iterrows():
    cx, cy = row.geometry.centroid.x, row.geometry.centroid.y
    ax.annotate(
        row['Borough'],
        xy=(cx, cy),
        fontsize=7, color='#2C3B4D',
        fontweight='bold', ha='center', va='center',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.2',
                  facecolor='#F7F4ED',
                  edgecolor='none', alpha=0.7)
    )

# ── Legend for highlights ────────────────────────────────────────
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
legend_elements = [
    Line2D([0], [0], color='#A35139', linewidth=1.8,
           linestyle='--', label='Top 5 underpredicted boroughs'),
    Line2D([0], [0], color='#2C3B4D', linewidth=1.8,
           linestyle='--', label='Top 5 overpredicted boroughs'),
]
ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, fontsize=8,
          framealpha=0.8, labelcolor='#1B2632',
          loc='lower left', facecolor='#F7F4ED',
          edgecolor='#C9C1B1')

# ── Bounds ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
minx, miny, maxx, maxy = gla_plot.total_bounds
pad_x = (maxx - minx) * 0.02
pad_y = (maxy - miny) * 0.02
ax.set_xlim(minx - pad_x, maxx + pad_x)
ax.set_ylim(miny - pad_y, maxy + pad_y)

ax.set_title('Spatial residuals — Type A person/society crime',
             fontsize=11, fontweight='bold', color='#1B2632', pad=10)
ax.text(0.5, 1.005,
        'Rust = underpredicted · Blue = overpredicted · '
        'Dashed outlines = top 5 boroughs by mean residual',
        transform=ax.transAxes, ha='center',
        fontsize=8, color='#615D56', style='italic')
ax.set_axis_off()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Figure 14: Spatial Residuals — Type A Person and Society Crime Choropleth map of Random Forest residuals across 679 London wards. Rust indicates underprediction; blue indicates overprediction. Dashed outlines mark the top five boroughs by mean residual in each direction. Underprediction concentrates in outer east London (Barking and Dagenham, Redbridge) and deprived west London (Brent), consistent with socioeconomic deprivation driving crime beyond what POI density predicts. Kensington and Chelsea also underpredicts — likely reflecting high-value tourist targets rather than deprivation. Overprediction clusters in affluent outer south-west London, where high amenity density does not translate to elevated crime.

These findings are summarised below, along with limitations and directions for future work.

Limitations¶

[ go back to the top ]

Five limitations qualify these findings. First, police-recorded data systematically undercounts crime in deprived areas, likely compounding model underprediction there. Second, ward-level aggregation means findings describe area-level patterns only and cannot be attributed to individuals. Third, OSM coverage is uneven across London, potentially underrepresenting amenity density in outer boroughs. Fourth, the absence of temporal, demographic, and socioeconomic controls limits causal inference. Fifth, 25 wards in the 2024 ONS boundary revision had no matching MPS crime records, reducing the analytical sample from 704 to 679 wards. Despite these constraints, the consistency between CV and test performance suggests the core findings are robust to sampling variation.

Conclusion¶

[ go back to the top ]

This study demonstrates that open-source POI data alone can predict ward-level crime density with moderate to strong accuracy, without recourse to demographic or socioeconomic variables. The key finding is that amenity type matters as much as amenity density. Convenience stores and fast food are the primary generators of person and society crime; cafés dominate property crime prediction. Most strikingly, night-time economy venues — bars, pubs, and nightclubs — showed unexpectedly weak predictive power despite their prominence in criminological literature. This is not a null result: it suggests that the regulatory environment of licensed premises actively suppresses crime opportunity, and that sustained daytime footfall is a stronger generator of crime in London wards than night-time activity. These findings have direct practical applications — the model could be used to identify which wards are most vulnerable to specific crime types based on their amenity profile, enabling more targeted crime prevention and resource allocation without requiring sensitive demographic data.

The spatial residuals reveal where this account is incomplete. Systematic underprediction in deprived outer boroughs and overprediction in affluent ones suggests that socioeconomic factors operate independently of physical land use. POI density captures the physical opportunity structure of crime; socioeconomic variables capture the social conditions that generate it. These frameworks are complementary, and future work combining both has the potential to provide a more complete and actionable account of crime determinants across London.

In [44]:

print(f"Runtime: {elapsed/3600:.2f} hours ({elapsed/60:.1f} minutes)")

Runtime: 0.02 hours (1.1 minutes)

References¶

[ go back to the top ]

Boeing, G. (2017) 'OSMnx: New methods for acquiring, constructing, analyzing, and visualizing complex street networks', Computers, Environment and Urban Systems, 65, pp. 126–139.

Brantingham, P. and Brantingham, P. (1995) 'Criminality of place: Crime generators and crime attractors', European Journal on Criminal Policy and Research, 3(3), pp. 5–26.

Cichosz, P. (2020) 'Urban crime risk prediction using point of interest data', ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 9(7), 459. doi:10.3390/ijgi9070459.

Cohen, L.E. and Felson, M. (1979) 'Social change and crime rate trends: A routine activity approach', American Sociological Review, 44(4), pp. 588–608.

Malleson, N. and Andresen, M.A. (2016) 'Exploring the impact of ambient population measures on London crime hotspots', Journal of Criminal Justice, 46, pp. 52–63.

ONS Open Geography Portal (2024) Ward boundary polygons. Available at: https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk (Accessed: April 2025).
